pdf_content:
TSIS-1 TIM Release Notes for Version 03 

Data Product Description 
The Total Irradiance Monitor (TIM) measures the total solar irradiance (TSI), monitoring the 
incident radiant energy powering the Earth’s climate system. The TIM uses an ambient-
temperature active cavity radiometer providing long-term repeatability with estimated 
uncertainties of approximately 0.014 W/m^2/yr (10 ppm/yr). Accuracy varies with time 
depending on instrument operating conditions and on-orbit effects, such as degradation due to 
solar exposure. Estimates of the TIM's time-dependent uncertainties are provided in the data 
file. The available Level 3 TSI data products produced by the TSIS-1 program consist of daily and 
6-hourly average irradiances reported at a mean solar distance of 1 astronomical unit (AU) and 
zero relative line-of-sight velocity with respect to the Sun. At-Earth values are also included for 
climate researchers to use as inputs to models. 

Two TSI data files are produced daily. One contains the daily means and the other the four 6-
hourly means. Due to the small size of the data files and to maximize ease of use to end-users, 
each delivered TSI product contains science results for the entire mission. Lower-level data 
products (e.g. Level 2) have limited scientific value due to frequent gaps caused by the Earth 
and/or the ISS itself occulting the Sun in the TSIS-1’s low Earth orbit, and are therefore not 
publicly available from the GES DISC. Short time ranges of these data are, however, available 
upon request for specific, directed science-focused efforts. 

Data Quality Description 
On-orbit instrument characterization is an on-going effort. The TIM team regularly tracks 
instrument degradation and calibrates the instrument servo system on-orbit, periodically 
updating the data processing system with new calibration values.  

Data Access 
TSIS-1 data products are available for public access. TSIS-1 data may be accessed from three 
different locations:

 Data Availability 
Temporal gaps in the daily and 6-hourly averages frequently occur due to ISS activity (e.g., orbit 
boosts), ISS anomalies (e.g., payload power outages), extreme solar beta angles causing local 
obscurations and/or glint, and instrument calibration/configuration experiments. At the time of 
the initial V.03 data release (2018-01-11 to 2020-05-08), 87.2% of the daily and 73.6% of the 6-
hourly samples are available. 

Latest release of the TSIS-1 TIM total solar irradiance (version 03).

Data Version 03 Notes 

•  Third public release of TSIS-1/TIM data 
•  The calibration updates in this release have decreased the uncertainties in prior versions 
that were largely due to intra-instrument cavity-to-cavity differences. The TSIS-1/TIM 
continues to agree well with the lower TSI values first reported by the SORCE/TIM and 
the follow-on TCTE/TIM. 

•  Updated time-dependent Level-3 uncertainties are included in the data products 
•  Updated non-equivalence ratio calibrations are derived from an analysis of Level-2 data 
products processed utilizing DC Subtraction, an alternative to the current processing 
algorithm, Phase Sensitive Detection.  

•  Updated servo gain calibration 
• 
• 
•  Minor software fixes and performance improvements 

Implemented a linear time-dependent degradation correction for the primary cavity 
Internal software updates to support alternative processing algorithms (on-going)
